sentences.text: Do I have to look there, or you stop for a while?
Excuse me, pardon?
Which names?
His parents, no, not at all.
But I tell you something else.
I forgot about it.
About the life in her place.
She used to entertain people, because she was so very wealthy, and such a big farm.
Very wealthy, she was.

The government [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] used to assign people from war, [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] like SS men or from Gestapo-- I don't know what he was-- always a person to stay with for a holiday, if they were not too well, with a family who had a big house, could give them a room, and food, and everything.
So they assigned for her [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] one-- I think he was SS-- very ugly person, in uniform, of course.
He was in that room opposite her bedroom where her husband, when he came home, he was there.
I can see it opposite her room.
I was [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] in the house, not as a maid, but [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] to care for the children.
When he came, [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] he left for the night.
He was very dirty, terribly dirty.
He used to spit on the floor-- polished beautiful-- it was a beautiful home, polished always-- to spit on the floor or even to clean his nose on the floor.
[PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] I couldn't take it.
[PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] Then he put out his boots for the night for me to clean them for him.
For him, I was a forced labor girl, a girl who should do anything.
As he came from such a high rank, SS man, whatever he was, I should clean his boots.
I told her-- it was silly of me, but still, my pride.
I said, I'm not going to clean his boots.
She said, Wanda, you must.
Because anything he could say, no one would refuse.
Only [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] you must do it.
Do it.
I understood, and did it.
Two days later, he said to her-- she told me, he said-- she told me.
She didn't say that she knows that I am Jewish.
But that was after the hospital.
And she said, Wanda, [GERMAN].
It means that she knew that I am Jewish.
And she paid for me hundreds and hundreds and hundreds of [GERMAN], German marks.
And not only she had to give them hundred dozens of-- hundred more every month of eggs and chickens and anything just for the hospital, for the people there, for saving me, for letting me live.
That's why I have her on my conscience.
[PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS]
When he said, about two days later, I was lucky, she said, Wanda, you know what he said?
He said-- in German, he said-- I don't know how to translate.
He said, [GERMAN].
It means, I break my head, my mind, to find out who Wanda is, he said, the man who was there.
She didn't wait for me to ask her.
She said, and I told him, I think she's of-- because I look different.
I think she is of French descent.
[PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] She told him this.
She rescued me many times.
I-- she told me this.
I-- she didn't wait for me to ask, what did you say in there?
But she said herself, she said, and you know, I told him-- still not talking, you know, openly with me, still not letting me know that I am Jewish.
She said, and I told him.
And he said, I, I break my, my mind.
That's it translated, but not exactly.
You would translate it better-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to, to know, to find out who Wanda is.
And I told him, she said to me, that I think she's of French descent.
Because different eyes.
They all had blue eyes, the Poles there, all of them.
And very fair, white, nearly white, like, like, field, in the field, you know, hair.
You know, straight hair, nearly white, most of them.
So I said, I think she is of French descent.
So if she, as a German, a Gestapo man's wife, said that, so he believed her.
But I was lucky.
Two days later, he left.
He was called back.
He left.
Upstairs were, as guests-- they fled from another place of Germany-- her in-laws.
It means her husband's parents.
Old people, very fine people, husband and wife.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they stayed there.
I don't know.
I haven't-- probably she sent them up some food.
They never came down.
They were against their sons.
They were [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] very nearly sick of it.
Once, she said, they never go out of the house, his parents.
They are very fine people.
But they don't agree with Nazis.
And she used to give every Thursday dinners [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for top government officials.
They came, very wealthy people.
I don't know where they came from.
But she had to give it.
I don't-- I think it, it was a must for her to take them in.
I think they were all very wealthy, very elegant people, very well-dressed.
She had big rooms.
And when she took out her all-gold, you know, dinnerware, and [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] she took in from the, the wives of those people that worked in the fields for her.
And they cleaned for weeks the cutlery and everything else.
And she-- and another one was a cook and used to cook for-- and bake days and nights even for those people.
Then one day came in a hoard of people, a lot of people, and with horses.
And they sat in a huge dining room.
I've never opened the door to that dining room.
And they had big dinners.
But I had to come in, because they didn't-- she didn't have enough people serving them.
So [?
I ?] said, Wanda, if I can help out a little.
So there was another one who-- [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] like a waitress-- and told me only to help out.
As I came in there, into the-- very elegant people and everything was shining, you know, like you can see it like in picture sometimes, she-- not them, but she introduced me.
And she said, so here [GERMAN], my proud Pole, Polish girl, my proud-- [GERMAN], my proud Polish girl, how she introduced me.
When I was back, I went out.
It was through a hole opposite to the kitchen.
One man came in from them, I remember a big, quite a big man, very elegant, grayish, [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] and got out a whole pack of money and said, Wanda, have it.
I don't know if she told them or not.
I don't know.
All these people were against Hitler, all of them, all against.
She told me later.
Otherwise, they were all her friends.
And gave me a big [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] bundle of money.
I didn't want to take it first.
But he said, you know, I was serving them.
Take it.
Take it.
I took it.
I took it.
But the German money burned my hand.
I couldn't look at them and I couldn't-- I served, and I looked-- and I didn't look at them.
I didn't know who they were.
She told me later that they were against Hitler all.
And I opened-- there was a stove, not like our electric or gas.
You put in coal, whatever, you know, open the little door and burn it.
And on top was an iron top.
And you cooked on it.
That was in old times.
I opened this.
It was a [? fire. ?] I want to throw in the whole bundle of money.
I didn't need any money then.
And it burned me.
And I didn't want from Germans money.
And I didn't know who they are.
I knew only that because of them, I am here.
And because of them, my whole family was murdered.
And who knows if I will see my child ever.
She came in the last minute and said, Wanda, what are you doing?
I said, I don't want it.
Why didn't you give it to me, she said.
It's a lot of money.
Why don't you give it to me?
I never forget that.
But it burned.
She want to get it out.
She couldn't.
She wants the money for herself.
She said, such a lot of money.
You don't know how much they gave you.
I said, I don't want it.
I never wanted money from them.
She said, but they're good people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She didn't want to tell me.
Later, much later, she told me.
She didn't talk of Nazis at all, only said they are good people.
That's what happened to me there.
And the day-- [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] only at nighttime in my room, I cried, because I didn't know what happened to my child.
Partly I knew what happened to my family.
I knew before.
I knew how my sister gave herself up with a little child.
Only don't know what happened to her, and how they killed her, and how that little child, the little boy of five, her first child-- I know nothing.

And I don't know what-- I know what happened because a person who survived when I was years later from Australia, from here, to Germany-- to Israel, I met someone whom I knew before the war from Grodek, where my parents died.
He told me only that he knew how they marched them all to a forest where they were all killed.
But I don't know if my mother and father ever reached that forest.
If they didn't fell-- and I'm quite sure-- but I don't know.
No one told me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I'm sure that they fell before and other people walked over them.
And the Gestapo didn't allow anyone to help anyone if they fell.
Because for weeks, they didn't eat anything.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my mother wasn't a very strong person, neither my father.
They were not like other people.
[PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] They were slim and tall.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother survived.
And my little brother of 13 was beaten so long until-- and he screamed, mama.
He thought mama can help him.
But mama was, was made to look, not to move from where she looked how they beat him until he-- [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] he couldn't call anymore.
Compared with others, no.
It was only hard to think.
And I couldn't make myself not to think what happened to all the others.
And I thought that if I survive, I will be the only Jewish soul [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Europe.
[PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] I thought so, that I will be the only one.
What I have seen before, I was sure that, that all Jews were killed.
I didn't believe that anyone will survive.
And I didn't know then of those concentration camps.
We never knew anything about it until after the war, we found out.
We didn't know.
We knew only that they are killing them all.
No.
I wasn't hungry.
I'll tell you why.
First of all, know Germans are very stingy, all, all Germans.
Even as rich as they are, stingy as they are.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When she fed those people every Thursday, every week, hoards of them came to her place.
They were city people.
And she had a farm.
So in the city, it was different.
There was not enough food in Germany then.
Only the top government people had it.
Or in the armies, they fed them.
They needed them.
But otherwise, even Germans didn't have enough, because everything went to the army.
And-- but she was wealthy, the woman with whom I stayed.
And [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] she gave what I had.
As I said, the Poles, she never gave them anything.
I don't know.
Maybe she gave them some bread.
I don't know about it.
I wasn't interested, and I never asked.
I just lived from day to day, not thinking of anything what went around me.
I wasn't interested there in anything.
I was only one-- I think my mind worked in one-- [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] only one thing interested me.
What happens there all to those who are left?
And I tried to imagine where they are and how they killed them.
And I believed that no one survived, only myself.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] even if I didn't have, I wasn't hungry.
If I was hungry, I went to the Poles and ate potatoes from that big-- like, it's not an urn.
I don't know how to call it.
I can see it.
I can draw it, what it was-- for pigs, they cooked potatoes.
But because they worked in the fields, they could get out anything else.
They could get out a carrot.
Or they could get any other vegetables and cooked it for themselves.
They were allowed to cook for themselves whatever they could.
But they didn't give them anything.
And inside, in the house, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the cook was there all the time, because she cooked three times daily.
[PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] And I was there.
The cook had her husband [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] in-- in the other-- there, in the other building, with the other people.
He worked in fields.
She went late, about 10 o'clock, or 11:00 even late, to her room, to her husband there.
Otherwise, she was all the time here with me.
And she was a youngish woman, I don't know, about my age or maybe a year older.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she cooked.
But they never gave us from that what they cooked, never.
[PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] They gave us only bread for breakfast with jam and coffee.
That was for breakfast, nothing more.
Although she ate in, often, the kitchen.
And I have seen.
But she never shared with us anything.
She had eggs.
And she had butter.
Nothing for us, no.
For that woman-- although she cooked it.
But we sat at the table.
She gave us bread and jam and coffee.
That were-- black coffee.
Never seen an egg there, never.
So it sometimes, sometimes it was on a special day that she gave us just a tiny bit of chicken that that other woman cooked for the other.
That was then when she had those visitors.
So it was left sometimes, a little piece of-- otherwise, she didn't feed me.
And I was much thinner then.
I was very thin after the war, very thin.
And--
No.
But what I ate-- and that helped, that helped me a lot.
And I had a lot of calcium.
She had outside, she made-- she had her cows, a lot-- sheds of-- I don't know how many.
And those people worked there.
And she made cheese.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to make cheese, I don't know how she made it.
There were big tubs outside.
This-- [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] not hard cheese, like cottage cheese, inside big, big tubs.
And she had some machines to make the cheese later.
So she never watched us.
Other people too, but they didn't come.
That was on the backyard where I was in that building.
So with my hand, I got out a hand of cheese whenever I was hungry-- of that cheese.
Also it was like cottage cheese, soft.
And that what I ate.
And that, that was quite good.
Oh, I thought if my child would be here, I could-- I couldn't swallow it because I thought of her.
She's suffering hunger, then I eat it.
And that was better than anything else.
I had cheese.
No milk, but cheese.
--to let me live.
After being about three weeks there, [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] she suspected something.
[PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] See, an ordinary woman wouldn't suspect, wouldn't think so much.
I came to work.
I work.
She was very educated.
She had tertiary education.
[PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] And she was quite clever apart from this, a very happy person.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I think, from the first time, she suspected something, but didn't say anything.
[PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] One day after being there about between two and three weeks, it was, at the beginning, she said, Wanda, [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] make yourself ready.
We are going to Meiningen.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She didn't wait for me to, to ask, because she knew maybe I will not ask.
I was afraid.
Maybe it's a wrong question.
She said, all farmers around who has those people like you from Poland or other countries-- she didn't want to say forced labor.
She didn't say that-- but have those people, we have to go to a hospital to Meiningen.
I didn't ask.
Again, I didn't want to ask.
I understood.
I didn't know exactly what for to a hospital, but I am not sick.
And the other people are not sick.
Why do we have to go to a hospital?
And I thought to myself now, if till now I survived, that's my-- that will be my last time here.
And I, I, I even wanted to write a letter to my daughter, to the woman.
But I was afraid, if I write-- I had it in my mind what to write, that I, I, I am afraid I will never come back.
And I cried.
I suspected something very bad.
Why to a-- I couldn't understand it.
Why to a hospital?
And that was like, it came out later, a trick, which [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] they did before to find out who is Jewish.
That was it.
The Gestapo found out in Germany.
How, I don't know.
I'm quite sure.
I have heard it later, that-- later on that there were on false papers more people like myself in Germany brought in under-- [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] on this forced labor.
And they killed.
Because they found out, they had to do something about it, to find out who-- if they have more Jews in Germany among those people brought in.
They invented something.
And I asked here a doctor.
I found out that I'm right.
It was a blood test.
As we came-- no, she told me this.
But I had a stony face.
On the inside, I was scared.
I thought, it's the last day today, my-- that I will be killed now.
But I didn't show anything outwards.
And we went.
So we went.
The others went with some carriages, horses and carts, all the people.
I went-- she took me in her-- it was in beautiful, shining horses and a elegant carriage.
We came to Meiningen, to a big hospital.
I've never been there before.
And I haven't seen it.
We went in.
As we went in, she went into the office somewhere there.
And they had to sign some documents how many people she brought in.
And as I said before, she had about 30 people on her farm including me-- left me in a queue.
And she said, Wanda, like others, you go in there.
In the hospital, there was one room.
I think a laboratorium or what, pathology what it was.
And I liked always to be the last, like in prison, or like when we went in-- [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] when we were sent to Germany.
And I waited.
One after one we went in.
It took a little time.
And I haven't seen them coming back.
So I thought there must be the other side a door.
Go in here, and get out on the other side.
Finally came to me.
When it came to me, she appeared and went in with me.
[PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] I think she had to give them my papers, whatever it was signed when she took me from that [GERMAN].
We went in.
There was a doctor behind a desk.
[PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] And he took, normally, as you take blood for a test from a finger.
They didn't need much blood-- not from the vein, but from top finger-- and put it into blotting paper.
You know, you put it on blotting paper.
I looked.
I wanted to see.
Even if I have to die here, I want to see what it is.
I looked.
And I have seen as he took it, she looked at it too.
And it was a very dark, nearly black spot of that blood on the blotting paper.
She screamed out, Wanda, [GERMAN]!
Wanda, you have dark blood!
[PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] And then I fainted.
I knew that-- I understood that must be a trick.
But it is what she said.
It was dark blood.
I didn't think much, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because if I would think, I would come to the same conclusion.
They're going to kill me.
So I fainted and fell to the floor.
Till then, I never faint.
My life, I never faint, fainted.
Once when I took my child after kilometers walking to the hospital.
And second time, I fell.
When I woke up, I was in another room on those field beds.
And she said-- [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] I came to myself.
And she said, Wanda, don't worry.
That's it.
That's why I can't forgive myself is that I didn't do it till now.
We are going home.
Everything will be all right.
She didn't say, you are Jewish.
She didn't say anything.
She just said, don't worry.
We are going home.
And everything will be all right.
She settled it with him, I found out later, for hundreds and hundreds or maybe more Deutsche Mark and products from the farm-- chickens and cheese and milk and meat, whatever.
[PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] Went out, never a word, nothing.
It went on as it was before.
She had a friend who was assigned, I think, by the government, because all men were in the field, in the war.
But those men who had farms and paid a lot to the government were staying home.
But they were assigned to help others, women, whose husbands' farms-- whose husbands were in the war, and the women couldn't carry on the whole farm.
Hers was one of the largest.
She was very wealthy.
So he was assigned to help her out.
